chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents and Solutions

flashcards.q: Define a solution.
flashcards.a: A solution is a uniform mixture formed when one substance (solute) completely dissolves in another (solvent), creating an evenly distributed composition.

flashcards.q: What is a solute?
flashcards.a: A solute is the substance that dissolves in a solvent. It is usually present in smaller amount and disappears completely in the solution.

flashcards.q: What is a solvent?
flashcards.a: A solvent is the substance in which a solute dissolves. It is usually present in larger amount and acts as the dissolving medium.

flashcards.q: Define a mixture.
flashcards.a: A mixture is a combination of two or more substances. Mixtures can be uniform (solutions) or non-uniform (components not evenly distributed).

flashcards.q: What is a uniform mixture?
flashcards.a: A uniform mixture is a mixture in which components are evenly distributed throughout, having the same composition everywhere. A solution is a uniform mixture.

flashcards.q: What is a non-uniform mixture?
flashcards.a: A non-uniform mixture is a mixture in which components are not evenly distributed. Different parts may have different compositions and components may be visible.

flashcards.q: How do you identify solute and solvent in a solid-liquid solution?
flashcards.a: In a solid-liquid solution, the solid is the solute and the liquid is the solvent.

flashcards.q: How do you identify solute and solvent in a liquid-liquid solution?
flashcards.a: In a liquid-liquid solution, the component present in larger quantity is the solvent, and the component present in smaller quantity is the solute.

flashcards.q: What is air?
flashcards.a: Air is a gaseous solution consisting of approximately 78% nitrogen (solvent), 21% oxygen, and about 1% argon, carbon dioxide, and other gases (solutes).

flashcards.q: Why does ORS taste the same throughout when mixed?
flashcards.a: ORS tastes the same throughout because it is a uniform mixture where sugar and salt are evenly distributed at the molecular level.

flashcards.q: Define a saturated solution.
flashcards.a: A saturated solution is a solution in which the maximum amount of solute has been dissolved at a given temperature. No more solute can dissolve without changing the temperature.

flashcards.q: Define an unsaturated solution.
flashcards.a: An unsaturated solution is a solution in which more solute can still be dissolved at a given temperature. The solution has not yet reached its maximum dissolution capacity.

flashcards.q: What happens when you add solute to a saturated solution?
flashcards.a: When you add more solute to a saturated solution, the added solute does not dissolve. It settles as undissolved solid at the bottom despite stirring.

flashcards.q: Define concentration.
flashcards.a: Concentration is the amount of solute present in a fixed quantity of solution or solvent. Solutions are described as dilute (little solute) or concentrated (much solute).

flashcards.q: What is the difference between dilute and concentrated solutions?
flashcards.a: A dilute solution contains a small amount of solute in a given quantity of solvent. A concentrated solution contains a large amount of solute in the same quantity of solvent.

flashcards.q: Define solubility.
flashcards.a: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a fixed quantity of solvent (typically 100 mL) at a particular temperature.

flashcards.q: How does temperature generally affect solidity of solids in liquids?
flashcards.a: For most solids, solubility increases with temperature. As temperature increases, more solute can dissolve in the same quantity of solvent.

flashcards.q: What happens to a saturated solution when you heat it?
flashcards.a: When you heat a saturated solution, it becomes an unsaturated solution. Additional solute can then dissolve because the solubility increases with temperature.

flashcards.q: Why is temperature dependence of solubility important?
flashcards.a: Temperature dependence is important because hot water dissolves solutes more easily than cold water, which is useful in cooking and industrial processes.

flashcards.q: How does temperature affect gas solubility in water?
flashcards.a: Unlike solids, gas solubility in water DECREASES as temperature increases. More gas dissolves in cold water than in warm water.

flashcards.q: Why is dissolved oxygen important for aquatic life?
flashcards.a: Dissolved oxygen is essential for respiration of fish, aquatic plants, and other aquatic organisms. They cannot survive without dissolved oxygen.

flashcards.q: Why do aquatic organisms thrive better in cold waters?
flashcards.a: Cold water holds more dissolved oxygen than warm water. Aquatic organisms thrive better where dissolved oxygen content is higher.

flashcards.q: Why does carbonated soda taste the same throughout?
flashcards.a: Carbonated soda tastes the same throughout because dissolved carbon dioxide is uniformly distributed throughout the liquid, forming a uniform solution.

flashcards.q: Define density.
flashcards.a: Density is the mass of a substance per unit volume. Formula: Density = Mass / Volume. SI unit: kg/m³.

flashcards.q: What are common units for density?
flashcards.a: For liquids and solids in laboratory settings, common units are gram per millilitre (g/mL) and gram per cubic centimetre (g/cm³). SI unit is kg/m³.

flashcards.q: Why is water's density close to 1 g/mL convenient?
flashcards.a: Water's density is approximately 1 g/mL, making density calculations simple because relative density equals the numerical value of density in g/cm³.

flashcards.q: Is density an intensive or extensive property?
flashcards.a: Density is an intensive property. It does not depend on the size or amount of the object. All samples of the same substance have the same density.

flashcards.q: What factors affect density of a substance?
flashcards.a: Density depends on the substance type and environmental conditions like temperature and pressure, but not on shape or amount of material.

flashcards.q: Define mass.
flashcards.a: Mass is the quantity of matter present in an object or substance. Measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg) using a balance.

flashcards.q: Define volume.
flashcards.a: Volume is the space occupied by an object or substance. Measured in cubic metres (m³), litres (L), or millilitres (mL). 1 L = 1000 mL.

flashcards.q: What is the taring function on a digital balance?
flashcards.a: Taring subtracts the weight of the container from the total weight, allowing you to measure only the mass of the object itself.

flashcards.q: How is liquid volume measured in the laboratory?
flashcards.a: Liquid volume is measured using a measuring cylinder, a narrow transparent container with volume markings on the side.

flashcards.q: Why are measuring cylinders designed to be narrow and tall?
flashcards.a: Narrow and tall design maximizes accuracy. Small volume changes result in noticeable height changes on the scale, improving reading precision.

flashcards.q: What is a meniscus?
flashcards.a: A meniscus is the curved surface formed by a liquid in a measuring cylinder or container. For water, the meniscus curves downward (concave).

flashcards.q: How do you read a measuring cylinder with a clear liquid?
flashcards.a: For clear liquids like water, read the mark at the BOTTOM of the meniscus while positioning eyes at level with the meniscus.

flashcards.q: How do you read a measuring cylinder with a coloured liquid?
flashcards.a: For coloured liquids, read the mark at the TOP of the meniscus while positioning eyes at level with the meniscus.

flashcards.q: What is the displacement method?
flashcards.a: The displacement method is used to measure the volume of irregular solid objects by measuring how much water they displace when submerged.

flashcards.q: Why does the displacement method work?
flashcards.a: When an object is submerged, it pushes water aside by a volume equal to its own volume. Measuring water level change reveals the object's volume.

flashcards.q: How do you calculate volume of regular solid objects?
flashcards.a: For regular shapes like cubes and cuboids, measure length, width, and height, then calculate: Volume = length × width × height.

flashcards.q: Define relative density.
flashcards.a: Relative density is the ratio of a substance's density to water's density. Formula: Relative density = Density of substance / Density of water. It is unitless.

flashcards.q: When an object floats in a liquid, which is less dense?
flashcards.a: The object is less dense than the liquid. An object floats if its density is less than the liquid's density.

flashcards.q: When an object sinks in a liquid, which is denser?
flashcards.a: The object is denser than the liquid. An object sinks if its density is greater than the liquid's density.

flashcards.q: What is neutral buoyancy?
flashcards.a: Neutral buoyancy occurs when an object's density equals the liquid's density, causing the object to neither float nor sink.

flashcards.q: Why does oil float on water?
flashcards.a: Oil floats on water because oil has lower density (~0.9 g/cm³) than water (1.0 g/cm³).

flashcards.q: Why does iron sink in water?
flashcards.a: Iron sinks in water because iron has higher density (~7.9 g/cm³) than water (1.0 g/cm³).

flashcards.q: Why does ice float on water?
flashcards.a: Ice floats on water because ice has lower density (~0.92 g/cm³) than liquid water (1.0 g/cm³) at 0°C. This is anomalous behavior.

flashcards.q: Why does a raw egg sink in fresh water but float in salt water?
flashcards.a: A raw egg sinks in fresh water (density slightly > 1 g/cm³) but floats in salt water because salt water is denser than the egg.

flashcards.q: How does temperature generally affect density?
flashcards.a: Generally, density decreases with heating and increases with cooling. Heating causes particles to spread apart, increasing volume without changing mass.

flashcards.q: Why does hot air rise?
flashcards.a: Hot air has lower density than cool air. Lower density air rises in higher density air.

flashcards.q: What is water's anomalous density behavior?
flashcards.a: Water has maximum density at 4°C, not at lower temperatures. As temperature drops below 4°C or rises above 4°C, density decreases.

flashcards.q: Why is ice's lower density than water important for aquatic life?
flashcards.a: Because ice floats, it forms on the surface and insulates water below, keeping it relatively warm and allowing aquatic organisms to survive in frozen conditions.

flashcards.q: How does pressure affect gas density?
flashcards.a: Increasing pressure on a gas decreases its volume (compresses it closer), so density increases significantly.

flashcards.q: How does pressure affect liquid density?
flashcards.a: Pressure has minimal effect on liquid density because liquids are nearly incompressible. Volume changes very little under pressure.

flashcards.q: How does pressure affect solid density?
flashcards.a: Pressure has negligible effect on solid density because particles are already very tightly packed and cannot compress further.

flashcards.q: What explains why a flat wooden board floats despite wood being denser than water?
flashcards.a: The board's large surface area distributes weight and air pockets lower the overall average density of board-plus-air combination, allowing it to float.

flashcards.q: How do ships made of steel (denser than water) float?
flashcards.a: Ships float due to hollow construction that traps air. The average density of the ship-plus-air combination is less than water's density.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Solute + _____ = Solution.
flashcards.a: Solvent

flashcards.q: Fill in: In a saturated solution, _____ solute can dissolve at that temperature.
flashcards.a: no more

flashcards.q: Fill in: An _____ solution can dissolve more solute.
flashcards.a: unsaturated

flashcards.q: Fill in: Gas solubility _____ as temperature increases.
flashcards.a: decreases

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ is mass per unit volume.
flashcards.a: Density

flashcards.q: Fill in: For water (clear liquid), read the meniscus at the _____.
flashcards.a: bottom

flashcards.q: Fill in: Volume of irregular solids is measured using the _____ method.
flashcards.a: displacement

flashcards.q: Fill in: An object floats if its density is _____ than the liquid's density.
flashcards.a: less

flashcards.q: Fill in: An object sinks if its density is _____ than the liquid's density.
flashcards.a: greater

flashcards.q: True or False: Different parts of a uniform mixture have different compositions.
flashcards.a: False. All parts of a uniform mixture have identical composition.

flashcards.q: True or False: Solubility is the same for all solids at all temperatures.
flashcards.a: False. Solubility depends on the substance and temperature.

flashcards.q: True or False: More salt dissolves in hot water than in cold water.
flashcards.a: True. Most solids have higher solubility at higher temperatures.

flashcards.q: True or False: More oxygen dissolves in hot water than in cold water.
flashcards.a: False. Less oxygen dissolves in hot water; gases are less soluble at higher temperatures.

flashcards.q: True or False: Density depends on the size of the object.
flashcards.a: False. Density is an intensive property independent of size.

flashcards.q: True or False: A small block of iron has lower density than a large block of iron.
flashcards.a: False. Both have the same density because density is independent of size.

flashcards.q: True or False: A measuring cylinder is designed wide and short for better accuracy.
flashcards.a: False. Measuring cylinders are designed narrow and tall to maximize accuracy.

flashcards.q: True or False: All objects with mass greater than water will sink in water.
flashcards.a: False. It is density (mass per unit volume), not absolute mass, that determines sinking.

flashcards.q: True or False: Ice is denser than liquid water.
flashcards.a: False. Ice is less dense than liquid water, which is why ice floats.

flashcards.q: True or False: Pressure significantly changes the density of liquids.
flashcards.a: False. Liquids are nearly incompressible; pressure causes minimal density change.
